segment-text: Anna Boden & Ryan Fleck
OFFICIAL WHITE January 20, 2014 EXT. RIVERBOAT CASINO PARKING LOT - IOWA - NIGHT

Outrageous flashing lights sparkle in the reflection of the windshield of a 2003 Subaru station wagon. Slumped behind the wheel is GERRY KARTWELL (early-40s, mustached). He listens to Joe Navarro's audio book of "200 Poker Tells" on CD. JOE NAVARRO'S VOICE Ninety-eight. Furrowing of Forehead. Furrowing of the forehead is a good
seen when people are concentrating or trying to make sense of something. CLOSE ON Gerry, glassy-eyed, perhaps fighting back tears. JOE NAVARRO'S VOICE (CONT'D)
Gerry looks in his rearview mirror, contorts and relaxes his face in a tension-releasing exercise. He does this several times, until he's ready.
INT. CASINO BATHROOM - SAME
We are behind the shaggy hair of a bearded man. teeth, leans over the sink and spits. He brushes his
INT. RIVERBOAT CASINO ENTRANCE
Gerry steps through the entrance, nods to the SECURITY GUARD, who smiles. Hey Gerry.
Good luck tonight.
INT. CASINO BATHROOM
Still from behind, we see the bearded man apply deodorant under his shirt, then tuck the stick back into his leather duffel.
INT. POKER ROOM
Gerry enters the casino's poker room, steps up to the CASHIER, hands over his player's card and sixty bucks. Hey Gerry.
How's the knee?
It's gettin' there.
She hands him a printed ticket.
LOUISE (CONT'D)
He glances at his ticket and wanders over to his assigned table, where he joins several players awaiting the start of tonight's low-stakes tournament.
(to the dealer) Hey Chuck.
Whatta ya say there, Gerry?

Gerry nods and Chuck slides over a stack of chips. Also seated at the table are-- CLIFFORD HOUSTON (40s, wearing a visor and goatee, with a permanent cranky scowl). WILL (20s, wearing a hoodie and sunglasses, listening to iPod). "BLOODY MARY" KATE (60s, drinking a bloody mary). LARRY (30s, black, laid back). Gerry folds his cards for the first several hands. calm, casual banter at the table.
Anyone know the spread on the Hawkeyes game? Six points.
Gettin' hot out there.
It's just weather.
What does that mean, "It's just weather?"
Hot, cold... it's just weather, baby.
There is a
I like that. "It's just weather, baby."
Gerry and the others laugh. Meanwhile, Clifford has been raising Larry, and they reveal their cards. Clifford wins and reels in his chips.
Yeah, well, it's just poker, baby. How's that? You like that one?

We ZOOM past Gerry at the table to reveal the bearded drifter approaching in the background. This man is CURTIS VONN (30s, handsome with a devil-may-care attitude). We reverse ZOOM back to Gerry at the table, where the conversation continues...
Anyone see that rainbow yesterday? Missed it.
I drove to the end of a rainbow once. Yeah?
Wasn't nothin' there. into the trees.
Just faded out Curtis has now arrived at Gerry's table, pulls out a chair.
Table seven looks like heaven. everybody feeling tonight?
How is Not sure what to make of this guy, most folks just nod.
(passing him chips) Have a seat.
What's the word, people?
It's just poker, baby.
Hey, it's okay with me. some cards.
Let's play Chuck dishes out the next hand; everyone peeks at their cards.
Aces are good, right?

Curtis smiles, bets a stack of chips. Most players fold. Not Gerry. He raises. Without missing a beat, Curtis meets Gerry's raise. When the flop comes down with an Ace, the one other remaining player folds. But Gerry looks quietly at the still-smiling Curtis and calls bullshit, raising him once again. Curtis looks Gerry over, zeroing in on a MISSING BUTTON ON GERRY'S SHIRT. (It's an odd detail to focus on in a moment like this, but that's the kind of guy Curtis is.) He mucks his cards, and Gerry reels in the pot. Aces, huh?
Why would I fold Aces? MINUTES LATER.
A waitress comes by.
Anyone need any drinks?
Bloody Mary. Bourbon.
What is the bourbon?
Just the cheap stuff.
Make mine a Woodford.
I'll have to check if we have--
(to Gerry) --Hey friend, you want a Woodford?
The cheap stuff is fine with me.
Bring him a Woodford, too.
That's okay.
Don't worry, it's on me. Gerry nods thanks.
hands... The friendly banter continues over multiple
What's the bad beat jackpot at? CHUCK
When's the last time it hit? Long time.
I got a bad beat jackpot story... My old buddy, Tony Roundtree-- anyone know Tony?
Blank looks and head shakes.
Hosts a high rollers game every other month in New Orleans now. But I knew him when he was up in Detroit.
You from Detroit?
The bet comes around to Curtis, who nonchalantly bets a large stack of chips. The next two players fold.
Me? I'm from all over, but I knew Tony Roundtree up in Detroit. (MORE)
He loved to play poker, but he kept dodging my invitations to play in the casinos. Story goes like this... ON GERRY scrutinizing Curtis for a tell...
CLOSE ON CURTIS' details as he continues with the story: hand gestures, smile, hazel eyes... he shows no signs of stress. Gerry folds his hand, and Curtis rakes in the pot.

Apparently Tony had a little bit of a gambling problem before I met him. You guys know about the disassociated persons list? Not sure you have it in Iowa. But some states you can put yourself on this list if you're trying to quit gambling. Which means you are legally banned from all casinos in the state. For life.

For life. So you know what I'm talking about. It makes sense, right? Well, not to Tony. He put himself on the list to show his girlfriend he was serious about changing his ways. Of course, a few months go by and she dumps him for a guitar player.
It's always a guitar player.
It is always a guitar player, isn't it?
Why is that? Nice hands.
So he's lonely and depressed and gets the itch to play poker again. Walks to the MGM-- "Sorry buddy." Greektown-"no can do, amigo." Motor City-- "no
dice, pal." So what does he do?
Yes! He puts on sunglasses, fake
mustache-- a wig.
C'mon, a wig?
I've never been more serious. He makes it past security, takes a seat in the poker room, and he's in heaven. What a rush that must have been. He's finally
back in action, and-The waitress returns with a tray of drinks.
I'm sorry, they don't have Woodford. Our top shelf is Bulleit.
She sets the bourbon glasses in front of Curtis and Gerry.
Okay, fine... but I'm not paying for his. I'm sorry?
I said his Woodford is on me. not a Woodford. Awkward beat.
Curtis smiles.
I'm only kidding... Keep the change, darlin'.
Curtis takes a moment to enjoy the ritual of a toast. his glass up to Gerry, who does the same.
Good luck to you, my friend. Thanks.
They sip their drinks.
Better than the cheap shit, right?
Gerry shrugs, nods politely. He holds
So what happened? Beat.
Curtis transitions back into story mode.
He's at the table for a couple hours, playing the best poker of his life. Up four, five thousand, when some guy's straight flush beats another guy's four cowboys.
Everyone at the table knows what this means and laughs. Jackpot!
Hundred fifty thousand dollar bad beat jackpot. To be split between all five players at the table.
Thirty grand a piece.
Thirty grand a piece. Unless you happen to be a disassociated person who is breaking the law by just being in the room. So while everyone else is high-fiving and back-slapping, Tony
jumps up, leaves his chips on the table, and makes a bee-line for the nearest exit... And he hasn't stepped
Curtis sips his bourbon. Gerry smiles.
And what is the lesson here?
The lesson? Lady, lighten up, it's just a story.
INT. DIVE BAR - LATER THAT NIGHT
Gerry enters the bar with a bounce to his step. A college basketball game plays on the TV. The bartender, DALE, greets him.
Hey Gerry, how'd it go tonight? Placed third.
What's the payout on that?
Nine hundred. What's the score here? Not sure.
Have a seat.
Gerry straddles a bar stool, scans the TV screen for a score.
Oregon up six. Okay.
That's okay.
The usual for ya?
Uh... actually, do you have Woodford? Yeah.
Let's do one of those.
Gerry keeps his eyes on the TV. He winces. A player misses a free throw.
How much you got on this?
Dale pours the Woodford and Gerry takes a sip, nods. It's a Woodford kind of night.
What do you mean?
That guy over there shooting darts likes it too.
We follow Gerry's gaze over to the dart board, where Curtis tosses a dart, shrugs, and pays his opponent five bucks. Gerry smiles, calls out to Curtis-Hey!
Woodford man!
Curtis looks over, recognizes Gerry.
Next one's on me!
Curtis (a little tipsy) steps over to the bar, sits next to Gerry. They shake hands.
Name's Curtis. Curtis!
Like Tony.
More like Mayfield. I'm Gerry. Like Lewis?
No. Just Gerry. With a G. Short for Gerald... Like Ford, I guess.
How'd it go for you tonight? Not bad.
What's "not bad"? Second.
Get outta here. That's great.
Could have finished first if you didn't bully me out of that mountain before you left.
Win some, lose some.
Hey, where'd you disappear to anyway?
I can never last through those tournaments.
Then why'd you buy in?
I like people.
Well you should've stuck it out. You're a good player.
For a few hours, maybe, but I don't have the stamina for poker. I get restless.
So what's your game? Darts.
You any good?
I can hit the board.
Hey, I gotta ask... what were you holding?
Curtis smiles, pauses for effect.
Pair a deuces.
Gerry stares at him, always looking for the tell.
That's all?
Is this guy for real?
I can usually read people like a damn book. But you were all over the map my friend. I still can't spot your tell.
You know why?
Gerry waits for it.
I don't care about winning... Bullshit!
I just like to play.
Curtis shrugs.
Gerry watches him in wonder, then turns back to
You see that rainbow yesterday?
Gerry stares at Curtis.
Is he serious?
Amazing! It was fucking amazing!
Best - rainbow - ever.
Hey, why have I never seen you before?
I'm just passing through town.
Headed where?
New Orleans.
I love New Orleans! You going to your friend's high roller game?
Tony Roundtree. Nah, I don't have that kind of money.
So, what then?
What do you mean?
You're just going down to New Orleans for the hell of it?
Pretty much.
Damn, that sounds alright.
It's alright with me.
Gerry and Curtis are hunched over the bar, drunk as hell, Gerry in the middle of describing a memorable poker hand.

I knew he had my two pair beat. But I also knew that he didn't know he had me beat by the way he was betting. I had four or five already on the line, so I figure, what the hell, and I push the rest of my chips in. He sits there for five minutes. Just staring at me. So I say "Tell ya what. For fifty bucks, I'll show you one of my cards, whichever one you wanna see."
Curtis breaks into a smile.
And whichever card you flip...
...he thinks I got a full boat. Beautiful!
Yeah, it really was.
Beat.
Gerry takes a quarter out of his pocket.
Twenty bucks... heads or tails? Tails.
Gerry flips the coin.
It's tails.
He hands Curtis a twenty.
Where did you come from?
I'm from a far away land where rainbows run free and whiskey grows on trees. ON GERRY for a bewildered beat.
He almost believes him. LATER. Gerry and Curtis drunkenly play darts, missing the board entirely, laughing.
INT. CLUTTERED LIVING ROOM - NEXT DAY

Gerry is passed out on the couch with a blanket draped over him. Sun shines through the windows. The sound of silverware clinking stirs him awake. Gerry struggles to open his eyes, and sees a young woman, DENISE (20s) sitting down at the chair opposite him. She's wearing pajamas and sprinkling Quik chocolate powder over a bowl of ice cream as she watches the morning news. Gerry looks around, struggling to remember where he is, when Curtis passes through the doorway in the bg and stops upon seeing Gerry. He's naked, brushing his teeth.
(mouthful of toothpaste) Oh, good, you're up. You too.
Hey Denise, you got any coconut oil?
I have lotion. Nevermind.
Curtis disappears into the bathroom.
Are you his, uh...?
Just friends.
Thanks for the couch.
You want breakfast?
Curtis returns in a pink robe.
Dog races start at one.
What time is it?
Nine-thirty. Shit.
What's wrong?
I have work.
You have a job?
Gerry nods, puts on his shoes.
Where'd I leave my car?
Hang on... Look who's running in the sixth race.
Curtis hands a newspaper folded on the race schedule to Gerry.
Dynamic Rainbow! Are you kidding me? Is that not our dog? Look me in the eye and tell me that's not our dog!
Gerry looks over the schedule, tempted.
It's a good name.
It's a great name!
Standing up, Gerry instinctively pats his left pant pocket to check for his wallet. Nothing there. Curtis notices, picks up a wallet sitting on the coffee table and tosses it to him.
Gerry holds his wallet awkwardly, fighting against the urge to look inside. Go on.
Count it.
Gerry shakes his head.
Naw, I don't-C'mon.
It's okay.
He opens the wallet, quickly checks his cash and cards, then slips it into his left pocket.
Trusting a stranger, Gerry. one of those things.
It's just
INT. GERRY'S HOUSE - DAY

Gerry enters his home through the side kitchen door with a stack of mail. He flips on the light switch, but nothing happens. He turns the switch off and on again. Still nothing. He mutters to himself, but doesn't appear surprised that the electricity has been shut off.
Honey, I'm home.
A cat approaches him, meowing like crazy.
I know, I'm sorry, sweetheart.
He puts some food in her bowl.
INT. GERRY'S BATHROOM
Gerry sits on the toilet, when he notices the empty roll beside him. Still seated, he leans over, opens the cabinet beneath the sink. No extra rolls. GERRY. Shit.
INT. GERRY'S LIVING ROOM
Gerry changes his clothes in a hurry, when his cell phone rings. He checks the number, sees an incoming call from SAM, and declines it.
EXT. FRONT PORCH - DAY
Wearing dark shades to conceal his hangover, Gerry fumbles with a huge key ring outside the front door. He can't find the right key for this home's lock.
Okay, I think this is it...
He jams the key in and furiously wiggles it, but it won't turn. Nope.
We'll get there.
He tries another one.
I'm finally starting to understand what you're after. And I think this one's a real winner.
A WIDER ANGLE reveals a YOUNG COUPLE waiting on the porch with Gerry as he tries yet another key.
Sorry about this, folks.
They nod, politely.
INT. HOME FOR SALE - KITCHEN
While the newlyweds examine the kitchen, Gerry fixates on their interaction from the hall, trying to get a read on them. The couple speak in hushed tones.
I really wanted gas, honey.
Well, could we switch it out? I don't know. Where'd he go?
He's over there. MAN A gas range would be nice.

ON GERRY as phone vibrates. He turns away from the couple and checks an incoming text: it's a photo of Curtis at the dog track with the text reading "45 min to dynamic rainbow!" Gerry smiles, thinks for a beat, then-16
EXT/INT. GERRY'S CAR - DAY
Gerry drives on the highway talking to Curtis on his phone.
I'm on my way!
EXT. DOG TRACK GRANDSTAND - SAME
We intercut with Curtis at the track.
Our race is up in twenty minutes. What's your ETA?
About fifteen.
Okay, good. Do you want me to place a bet for you? They're saying Dynamic Rainbow has 48 to one odds. 48 to one?!
That's terrible.
That's what makes it fun, Gerry. I'm laying fifty to win. What do you want to do?
Geez, I don't know.
You can bet to place if you want.
How many dogs are racing?
First or second to place.
Uh, let me think...
Don't stress, baby; it's just dogs.
Put me down for two hundred to place.
Okay, see you soon.
Gerry sets down his phone and turns up his "200 Poker Tells" audio book on the stereo.
Number nine. Nodding approval when strong. Inadvertently, people will nod affirmatively when they realize they have the best hand. Sometimes it looks
like the person is just rocking.
EXT. DOG TRACK PARKING LOT - DAY
Gerry runs through the lot toward the main entrance.
INT. DOG TRACK GRANDSTAND

He enters the grandstand area, where a fantastic assortment of oddball characters are assembled on a weekday afternoon-- most of them over 60 and smoking. Gerry spots Curtis at the bar holding court with the regulars. He has them laughing and wheezing.
Gerry, you made it! (handing Gerry his half full cup) Have a beer.
How's it going?
We're up next. Our dog is so beautiful, Gerry. They walked it through, you should have seen it. Good legs?
Great legs. Strong, but agile. real winner.
He's a
I can't remember the last time I've been to a dog track.
But you love cats.
Am I right? Gerry stares at Curtis. How could he possibly know this? Curtis pinches a clump of cat hair off Gerry's coat. Gerry smiles, tries to brush off the remaining cat hair.
I'm more of a poker man. sports.
It's a sport for dogs. C'mon, let's get closer. (to the regulars) Good luck you beautiful losers!
Give'm hell, weirdo!
Curtis and Gerry stroll to the seating area.
Did you know Herbert Hoover was the first U.S. President born west of the Mississippi? I did not.
Right here in Iowa.
You win anything yet?
I'm only here for one race.

ON THE TRACK. The dogs step into the starting gate. GRANDSTAND. A commotion erupts behind Curtis and Gerry as betters cheer on a horse from the off-track-betting monitors. Drawn in by the excitement, Gerry turns around to watch. Curtis notices, pulls his attention back towards the dogs.
Be here now, Gerry. This is the only race.
HERE COMES RUSTY!!
A HORN sounds and the dogs sprint out of the gate, chasing a furry lure (Rusty) attached to a mechanical arm. Here we go!
The announcer provides a moment by moment description of the race as the dogs tear into the first turn.
I can't see. Where is he?
C'mon rainbow!
Coming out of the first turn it's
Goldilocks followed by Mystery Train.
As the other spectators cheer on their picks, a cacophony of voices shouting out different numbers nearly drowns out the announcer's voice.
Where is he? Which number's ours?
Number four!
Coming up on the outside is Dynamic Rainbow.
Dynamic Rainbow!
C'mon rainbow! C'mon four!
As the dogs come into the home stretch, we see dog #4 (Dynamic Rainbow) move up into second place. Gerry and Curtis go nuts, urging on their dog.
C'mon rainbow! Hot damn!
C'mon rainbow!
Dynamic Rainbow is now even with the leader as they approach the finish line. Gerry lets out a howl.
And it's Dynamic Rainbow by a nose. Curtis screams, hugs Gerry.
What did he say?
Curtis doesn't respond, but continues to dance in celebration, breaking into song...
"Somewhere over the rainbow..."

The realization finally sets in and Gerry joins in the celebration dance as if he just scored the winning touchdown of the Superbowl. He screams and points at the other spectators, which may go against proper track etiquette, but who cares? The triumph is short-lived, however, as Gerry is suddenly overcome with despair.
I fucking bet to place!
You still won big.
I'm an idiot! Why didn't I listen to you?
Gerry, we won. Let's go celebrate! We'll get some drinks, meet some ladies, go to a show...
Go where? I love this place. We can have drinks here, meet ladies here.
Gerry, Gerry, Gerry... It don't get any better than this. Let's go have some fun!
I just got here! Okay, listen to this... next person through that door-Gerry--
If the next person through that door is wearing glasses, we stay for one more. You're on.
They look over to the men's room door, where a gray-haired man with a cane and glasses, steps out. Gerry smiles.
INT. POOL HALL - NIGHT
Gerry and Curtis drink bourbon at the bar.
I'll tell you what, Gerry... never bet on a dog named after a disabled president.
I thought it was Teddy.
Rollin' Roosevelt?
It was a good name.
It was not a good name. Next time listen when I say it's time.
Gerry's phone registers a new message. it away, ignoring the text. He checks it, then tucks
There was this guy back in Kansas City, Archie Hannahan. Never knew when to quit.
I know where this is going and you don't need to tell me-CURTIS Listen, Gerry. Okay.

Archie owed every poker player in town at least a few hundred bucks. Got to the point where he was asking his little nephews for the change in their piggy banks. One day he's in the poker room at Harrah's. I walk over to say
hello, notice he's got a handsome pile of chips, over five grand. Archie starts bragging that he started the
blind comes around." I smile, walk
Archie's high-pitched voice saying... "I'm all in!"
And then he lost it all.
Of course he lost it all. But you know what he says to me? "Don't worry. It was only sixty bucks."
Some guys are just born to lose.
A burst of jubilation from the pool tables nearby draw Curtis and Gerry's attention.
A little, but not in a long-Before Gerry can finish, Curtis is calling out across the room.
Twenty bucks on my friend here. Hey!
What're you doing?
I'm gonna watch you play this big winner here. You said you played.
It's been a long time.
So you'll lose.
No, you'll lose.
I'm spending twenty bucks to watch you make a fool of yourself. That's not losing; that's entertainment.
Gerry smiles. Who does this guy think he is?
You want entertainment?
Gerry strolls over to the Big Winner's table.
Let's make it a hundred.
Curtis' demeanor suddenly shifts.
Whoa, hey, Gerry! Play nice now.
Big Winner smiles, racks up the balls, as Gerry chalks his cue, and leans over the table. Curtis sips his drink, then--
Five hundred!
Gerry pauses, looks to Big Winner who is now looking a little less confident. He nods in agreement anyway. Not to be outdone, Gerry stares down Curtis, then...
Let's play for a thousand.

Curtis smiles, loving it, but Big Winner (and his friends) are not amused. OTHER FOLKS at the bar also begin to take notice and step over. Gerry leans over the table, prepared to break, but Big Winner puts his hat over the cue ball-BIG WINNER That's enough. You fellas don't belong here, do you?
I'm really not very good at this.
Curtis steps over to the table, pulls Gerry away, while Big Winner and his tough guy friends give hard looks.
Let's go, Gerry. These guys don't want our money.
Curtis and Gerry head for the exit and we HOLD ON A SKINNY THUG watching them go.
EXT. POOL HALL - NIGHT
Curtis and Gerry stroll through the parking lot, laughing.
I can't believe you almost blew a thousand bucks of my money.
Are you kidding? I just saved you from losing twenty... So what are you up to tomorrow?
I think it's Macchu Picchu time for me. Say again?
Time for me to hit the road.
Gerry tries to conceal his disappointment. Okay.
Well, it was great to meet you.
You too, Gerry. Good luck.
They shake hands.
You need a ride someplace?
Nah, I'm just gonna steal a bike.
Gerry smiles, watches Curtis drift away, then wanders over to his car. He reaches into his pocket, fumbling with his keys, when the skinny thug from the bar approaches him with a cigarette in hand.
Hey man, you got a light? Beat.
Gerry looks the guy over.
Uh, no, sorry...
Suddenly, the thug pulls out a switchblade.
Aw man, what's that for?
Gimme the money.
I don't have any money.
Bullshit. Thousand dollar bets? heard you back there.
I can't believe this.
Just give it to me!
I told you, I don't have any money! lost it on Rollin' Roosevelt. Huh? What?
GERRY
Don't make me cut you. The thug threatens him with the knife. nervous.
Gerry is getting
Okay, just relax. Hurry up!
Gerry takes out his wallet. See? Flashes it. Empty. GERRY
Do I look stupid?

A burst of LAUGHTER breaks out from the pool hall exit. Startled, the thug reacts by stabbing Gerry in the belly (not deep) and booking it out of there. Gerry cups his stomach, then checks his hand, now bloodied by the wound. Damnit!
Gerry realizes the thug dropped his knife before running. picks it up.
You forgot this, asshole!
He throws the knife. Winces in pain. then climbs in the car.
INT. BLUEBERRY HILL DINER

Gerry gathers himself, Gerry steps into the diner, looks around, and spots SAM (Samantha, a gentle-looking woman in her 50s). She hugs Gerry like they're old friends having a reunion. Gerry hugs her back, but we sense his discomfort.
It's so great to see you, Gerry. long's it been?
Oh, I don't know.
A few months now.
Too long. It's been too long. been avoiding me, haven't you? No, I-- You've
The WAITER steps up.
(to Gerry) Can I bring you anything to drink?
Um, coffee, please.
Forgive me, I got here a few minutes early, and ordered a salad. But order anything you like. It's on me.
Oh, no, that's okay.
Of course, it's okay.
I insist.
Thank you. I'll have a burger, mediumrare. And fries. The waiter walks away.
And how are you?
Oh, you know, same old grind. I'd love to retire soon, but it feels like a long way off. How's the real-estate?
Slow and low.
That's too bad. up.
I'm sure it'll pick
Eventually.
A long, awkward pause. Gerry tunes in to the details of Sam's behavior: her nail CLICKING against her coffee mug, her FALSE SMILE, the narrowing of her eyes as she stares at Gerry.
Don't make me ask, Gerry... Gerry straightens up, as the waiter returns with a salad.
Unbelievable. Excuse me, I asked for the dressing on the side.
Oh, I'm sorry. Do you want me to take it back?
I want you to hold it between your knees.
The waiter stares at her, confused.
(to Gerry) He doesn't get it... Yes, take it back, please.
The waiter takes off with the salad, and Sam calls after him.
I'm here three days a week and every time with the dressing. Oh, boy. What're you gonna do?
What're you gonna do? Another odd beat.
An uncomfortable tension is mounting.
Okay, look-- I had eight hundred last night and some meth head robbed me by knife point and stabbed me in the gut.
He lifts up his shirt, revealing the blood-stained bandage.
Oh, god, you should have that checked out. Are you okay? Yeah.
Thankfully.
I can't imagine.
It was pretty scary.
Well, at least you're okay.
Just a scratch.
Could have been a lot worse ya know? Beat.
Gerry sips his coffee, recognizing Sam's veiled threat.
So what did you bring me today?
Gerry nervously opens his wallet, hands over everything in it.
That's about eighty. I'll have more for you next week. I mean I had eight --the meth head.
The fucking meth head.
It was a bad beat. The worst.
Things'll look up.
They always do.
Well... not always.
Another threat. Sam's salad.
Another tense beat. The waiter returns with
Sorry about that.
That's okay. I'm sorry about that knees comment. It's from a movie. couldn't resist.
Sam's phone RINGS.
Do you mind?
Gerry shakes his head. Sam answers.

(into phone) Yeah... Oh geez, c'mon Laura... I can't bring it to you at school, I'm in a meeting right now... Call your dad... How should I know?... It's not my fault you forgot it... Maybe you shoulda thought about that before you went out last night...
She covers the phone and whispers to Gerry.
Gerry puts on a false smile.
(into phone) Okay, you know what, I'm gonna do this for you, but this is the last time... Do you hear me?... Okay, sweetie, I'll be by in half an hour... Don't keep me waiting out front either...
Sam hangs up, returns her gaze to Gerry.
You're lucky you don't have to deal with this shit, Gerry, I tell ya. I hear ya.
What am I gong to do with you?
Next week, Sam. Don't worry about it.
Tomorrow, Gerry. you a visit.
I'll have Tim drop
Oh, hey, that won't be necessary... No need to bring him into this. If you could just give me a few more days, I have a commission coming in-SAM --Get it together, Gerry.
Sam stands up and lays $40 of Gerry's money on the table. puts his hand over hers.
I had the money.
I'm sure you did.
This time it wasn't my fault.
I understand.
Sam yanks her hand back and starts walking away.
Just a few more days! Things are turning around for me. You'll see...
